title: New command: podmansh
body:
<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
podmansh: New command and confined login shell
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *lsm5* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [lsm5] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Please add a description of what this is trying to do? I really do not understand the use case here. As I read the code all this is doing is calling `podman exec -it podmansh /bin/sh`? So why do need this special handling here, is calling this command really to much? And instead of adding a bunch of duplicated go code why not just add a bash script called `podmansh` which calls the same exec command and is much simpler to maintain.

I believe having a bash script by default for a login shell could be considered dangerous. We need to worry about people executing signals and other types of attacks while logging in. I also believe that this can be simplified down from what we are doing now, to a very small support change.

But this has to be well documented.

Began creating a man page for this PR.
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1OXGMwcAtGkmqSHd4pyE4QF84QKpHT2Dl07oXkeu-6AY/edit
@lsm5 Should we ship podmansh as a subpackage podman-shell? or podman-sh or podman-podmansh

> @lsm5 Should we ship podmansh as a subpackage podman-shell? or podman-sh or podman-podmansh

we can do just `podmansh`, so: `%package -n podmansh`.

> > @lsm5 Should we ship podmansh as a subpackage podman-shell? or podman-sh or podman-podmansh
> 
> we can do just `podmansh`, so: `%package -n podmansh`.

And this could `Provides: podman-shell` , `podman-podmansh` etc.

Is it possible exec into the shell so that its cwd is user's home?

```
$ scp date podmanuser4@localhost:/home/podmanuser4
podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
date           

$ scp date podmanuser4@localhost:                 
podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
scp: dest open "./date": Permission denied
scp: failed to upload file date to .

$ ssh podmanuser4@localhost pwd    
podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
/
```
How would an user or an admin change user's shell? Currently it's set `/bin/sh` but Fedora defaults `/bin/bash`, and users might want to use zsh.

> Is it possible exec into the shell so that its cwd is user's home?
> 
> ```
> $ scp date podmanuser4@localhost:/home/podmanuser4
> podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
> date           
> 
> $ scp date podmanuser4@localhost:                 
> podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
> scp: dest open "./date": Permission denied
> scp: failed to upload file date to .
> 
> $ ssh podmanuser4@localhost pwd    
> podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
> /
> ```

I assume we have to set --workdir for that. Although I don't think we should do that. The podman exec default should be the same cwd as the one from the container run command. So I would argue you should set the correct dir in the quadlet unit?

ok - `PodmanArgs=--workdir` %h`  seems to work:

```
[Container]
# Image=registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora
Image=quay.io/plautrba/fedora-selinux
ContainerName=podmansh
RemapUsers=keep-id
RunInit=yes
PodmanArgs=--workdir` %h
```

```
$ ssh podmanuser4@localhost pwd
podmanuser4@localhost's password: 
/home/podmanuser4
```
a lot of fedora sites are flaky right now, including dist-git and registry. That's breaking some tests. Will retry later.

> > /lgtm
> 
> @bachradsusi I invited you as member to containers org. Further slash-commands should work after you accept it.

I don't think it's necessary, I just wanted to express that it looks good to me.
